01

The Big Picture

Before touching a single line of code, let's understand what this agent actually does — and why it exists.

Your personal hiring consultant. At superhuman speed.

You find a job listing. It looks promising — but between reading the job description, checking your resume for matching skills, writing a tailored cover letter, and guessing which ATS keywords might get you past the filter — you're looking at 30–45 minutes of focused work. For every application.

This project is an AI agent that compresses that entire process into seconds. You paste in a job URL or a block of text, and it hands you back everything you need to apply confidently.

💡 The Core Promise
One input (a job URL or text) → five structured outputs ready for your application. The agent does the reading, matching, writing, and gap-spotting so you can focus on the parts only a human can do.

The 30-minute problem every job seeker knows

Manual application prep isn't hard — it's just slow and repetitive. The same four steps, executed with full concentration, for every single role. This is exactly the kind of structured, repeatable work that software can automate.

1

Read & Parse the Job Description

Identify required skills, nice-to-haves, seniority level, and company culture signals buried in corporate jargon. Takes 5–10 minutes.

2

Match Skills Against Your Resume

Cross-reference every requirement with your background. Figure out your honest match percentage and which gaps are dealbreakers. Takes 10 minutes.

3

Write a Tailored Cover Letter

Draft something that directly addresses the role's priorities — not a generic template. Takes 10–15 minutes to get right.

4

Guess the ATS Keywords

Reverse-engineer which exact phrases the employer's screening software is looking for. Mostly guesswork without a tool. Takes 5 minutes.

⏱ Do the math
Applying to 20 jobs = up to 15 hours of prep work. The agent handles steps 1–4 in under 30 seconds, every time.

Five outputs. One request.

Every time you run the agent against a job posting, it returns five structured pieces of analysis. Each one is designed to be immediately useful — not raw AI output you have to interpret.

①

Match Score

A percentage showing how well your current resume aligns with this specific role's requirements. Helps you decide whether to invest time applying.

②

Skill Comparison Table

A side-by-side view of every required skill versus your demonstrated experience. Green = covered, amber = partial, red = gap.

③

Gap Analysis

Plain-language explanation of what's missing and how serious each gap is — with suggestions for how to address or frame them.

④

Cover Letter Draft

A fully written, role-specific cover letter that speaks directly to the job's stated priorities using language from the description itself.

⑤

ATS Keywords

The exact phrases and terms from the job description that ATS filters are most likely scanning for — ready to weave into your resume.

What a real session looks like

The agent runs from the command line. You give it a job URL or paste in raw text. Within seconds, it replies with the full analysis. Here's that conversation, step by step.

U
You

python main.py analyze --url https://jobs.example.com/senior-data-engineer

A
Agent

Fetching job description from URL...

A
Agent

Loading your candidate profile and selecting the best cover letter template...

A
Agent

Analysing job opportunity with Claude...

✓
Agent — Results Ready

Match Score: 78%
5 required skills matched · 2 gaps identified (Spark, dbt)
Cover letter drafted · 12 ATS keywords extracted
Files saved to: output/senior-data-engineer/

A
🗂 Output files, not just terminal text
The agent saves every result as Markdown and JSON files in a named output folder — so you can review, edit, and reuse the analysis at any time.

The entry point: where your command becomes action

When you run python main.py analyze, Python calls a function named cmd_analyze. This is the very first piece of real code that runs. Let's read it line by line.

CODE 

def cmd_analyze(args):
    if args.jd_file:
        jd_file = Path(args.jd_file)
        message = jd_file.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    else:
        message = args.input
print("\nAnalysing job opportunity...\n")
summary, files = handle_user_request(message)
print(summary)
PLAIN ENGLISH

def cmd_analyze(args): — Define the function that runs when you type the analyze command. It receives your flags and arguments as args.

if args.jd_file: — Did you pass a --jd-file flag pointing to a local text file? If yes, read that file's contents.

else: message = args.input — Otherwise, the job description came in as direct text (e.g. from a URL that was already fetched, or pasted inline).

print("Analysing...") — Show a progress message in the terminal so you know it's working.

handle_user_request(message) — Hand off the job description text to the main orchestrator. This one call triggers the entire analysis pipeline.

print(summary) — Display the final human-readable summary in the terminal when it's done.

🔀 Two input modes, one pipeline
Notice the if/else: whether you pass a file path or raw text, both paths produce the same message variable. The rest of the code doesn't need to care about where the text came from — a clean design pattern called input normalization.

Check your understanding

Your friend just found a job posting for a role they're excited about. They've heard about this agent and want to try it. Based on what you've learned, let's make sure the expectations are set correctly.

Your friend pastes in a job URL and runs the agent. What should they expect to get back?

Looking at the code, what does cmd_analyze do before calling handle_user_request?
